title: AGENTS.md
text: directory source tree ggmeta, R package extends ‘ggplot2’ build publication-quality forest plots meta objects tidy data frames. package root directory (subfolder). meta Suggests dependency, Imports: ggforest() works meta object meta installed, plain tidy data frame standalone. requireNamespace("meta") guards protect meta-dependent code paths. title: AGENTS.md
code: Layer 3  ggforest()                      high-level S3 generic (meta / data.frame)          theme_forest(), layout_jama(), layout_bmj(), layout_revman5() Layer 2  geom_forest_ci()      study CIs + weight-proportional squares          geom_forest_diamond() summary diamond (delegates to GeomPolygon)          geom_forest_ref()     vertical null-effect line          geom_forest_predict() prediction interval with caps Layer 1  tidy_meta()           meta object -> tidy data frame          fortify.meta()        ggplot2 S3 method wrapping tidy_meta()

title: AGENTS.md
text: Display order lives studlab factor levels, row order. Rows stay extraction order (stable inspection); factor levels encode top--bottom plot order (studies top, summary diamond bottom). ggforest.data.frame() pins y axis scale_y_discrete(limits = ...) order depend optional layers present. Back-transformation delegates meta::backtransf() every summary measure gets correct inverse (exp ratios, inverse-logit PLOGIT, Fisher-z ZCOR, etc.). hard-code exp() proportions/rates. detect_null_effect() returns 1 ratios, 0 differences/ correlations, NA single-group proportions/rates (reference line, log axis). Study weights use meta’s w.* slots usable, else fall back inverse-variance (1 / seTE^2); weight non-required aesthetic missing weight never deletes study’s CI. -plot text must avoid raw non-ASCII. heterogeneity caption built plotmath expression (italic()^2, tau^2) — embedding literal τ/² crashes R CMD check example rendering non-UTF-8 devices.

title: AGENTS.md
text: covr, vdiffr, spelling optional (Suggests) may installed locally; _R_CHECK_FORCE_SUGGESTS_=false lets check run without . R CMD check run built tarball, directory.
code: devtools::load_all()                 # load for interactive work devtools::test()                     # run testthat suite (tests/testthat/) devtools::document()                 # regenerate man/*.Rd + NAMESPACE from roxygen R CMD build .                        # build source tarball _R_CHECK_FORCE_SUGGESTS_=false \   R CMD check --as-cran --run-donttest ggmeta_*.tar.gz

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Display order lives studlab factor levels, row order. Rows stay extraction order (stable inspection); factor levels encode top--bottom plot order (studies top, summary diamond bottom). ggforest.data.frame() pins y axis scale_y_discrete(limits = ...) order depend optional layers present. Back-transformation delegates meta::backtransf() every summary measure gets correct inverse (exp ratios, inverse-logit PLOGIT, Fisher-z ZCOR, etc.). hard-code exp() proportions/rates. detect_null_effect() returns 1 ratios, 0 differences/ correlations, NA single-group proportions/rates (reference line, log axis). Study weights use meta’s w.* slots usable, else fall back inverse-variance (1 / seTE^2); weight non-required aesthetic missing weight never deletes study’s CI. -plot text must avoid raw non-ASCII. heterogeneity caption built plotmath expression (italic()^2, tau^2) — embedding literal τ/² crashes R CMD check example rendering non-UTF-8 devices.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: meta package: https://cran.r-project.org/package=meta ggplot2 extension guide: https://ggplot2-book.org/extensions R Packages book: https://r-pkgs.org/

what: The same object, a ggplot result
title: Coming from meta::forest()
text: meta::forest() draws directly graphics device grid-based layout. ggmeta::ggforest() takes meta object returns ggplot:  practical difference: everything ggforest() ggplot2. compose +, restyle theme(), add annotations, save ggsave().
code: library(meta) #> Loading required package: metabook #> Loading 'meta' package (version 8.5-0). #> Type 'help(meta)' for a brief overview.  m <- metabin(   event.e = c(14, 30, 15, 22), n.e = c(100, 150, 100, 120),   event.c = c(10, 25, 12, 18), n.c = c(100, 150, 100, 120),   studlab = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D"),   sm = "RR" )  # meta::forest(m)   # base-graphics forest plot ggforest(m)         # the same analysis, as a ggplot

title: Coming from meta::forest()
text: Relabel axis add title. ordinary ggplot2 calls:  Add effect / CI / weight columns — rightcols idea — columns:  custom column (say sample-size column ), reach geom_forest_text(). tidy_meta() exposes tidy data frame ggforest() builds internally, can align text rows:
code: ggforest(m) +   labs(title = "Risk of the event", x = "Risk ratio (log scale)") ggforest(m, columns = TRUE) td <- tidy_meta(m) studies <- td[!td$is_summary, ] studies$effect_txt <- format_effect(studies$estimate, studies$ci_lower, studies$ci_upper)  ggforest(m) +   geom_forest_text(aes(y = studlab, label = effect_txt), data = studies,                    x = 4.2, hjust = 0) +   expand_limits(x = 7)

title: Coming from meta::forest()
text: Reach meta::forest() want exhaustive, print-ready defaults box don’t need restyle. Reach ggmeta want ggplot can theme, compose, facet, drop existing ggplot2 workflow — tidy data frame effect sizes meta object (see vignette("getting-started")).

what: A forest plot from a meta object
title: Getting started with ggmeta
text: Fit meta-analysis usual, hand ggforest():  ggforest() sensible thing automatically: draws study confidence intervals weight-proportional squares, common- random-effects summary diamonds, prediction interval, null-effect reference line, log x-axis ratio measures, heterogeneity caption.
code: library(meta) #> Loading required package: metabook #> Loading 'meta' package (version 8.5-0). #> Type 'help(meta)' for a brief overview.  m <- metabin(   event.e = c(14, 30, 15, 22), n.e = c(100, 150, 100, 120),   event.c = c(10, 25, 12, 18), n.c = c(100, 150, 100, 120),   studlab = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D"),   sm = "RR" )  ggforest(m)

title: Getting started with ggmeta
text: don’t need meta package. data frame studlab, estimate, ci_lower, ci_upper works:
code: df <- data.frame(   studlab  = c("Trial 1", "Trial 2", "Trial 3", "Trial 4"),   estimate = c(0.82, 0.91, 0.68, 1.05),   ci_lower = c(0.61, 0.74, 0.48, 0.80),   ci_upper = c(1.10, 1.12, 0.96, 1.38) )  ggforest(df, null_effect = 1)

title: Getting started with ggmeta
text: Give ggforest() se column (let recover standard error confidence interval) set add_summary = TRUE pool studies inverse-variance common-effect DerSimonian–Laird random-effects model — without meta package:
code: studies <- data.frame(   studlab  = c("Trial 1", "Trial 2", "Trial 3", "Trial 4", "Trial 5"),   estimate = c(0.10, 0.35, 0.22, 0.48, 0.05),   se       = c(0.12, 0.10, 0.14, 0.16, 0.11) ) studies$ci_lower <- studies$estimate - 1.96 * studies$se studies$ci_upper <- studies$estimate + 1.96 * studies$se  ggforest(studies, add_summary = TRUE)

title: Getting started with ggmeta
text: Set columns = TRUE add familiar effect / 95% CI / weight columns headers right plot (use effect_header name estimate column, pass subset like columns = c("estimate", "ci") prefer):
code: ggforest(studies, add_summary = TRUE, columns = TRUE, effect_header = "SMD")

title: Getting started with ggmeta
text: column — sample sizes, events, anything — use geom_forest_text(), aligns study rows shared y. format_effect() builds “estimate (CI)” label. Widen panel expand_limits() make room:
code: studies$n <- c(120, 240, 150, 180, 110)  ggforest(studies, add_summary = TRUE) +   geom_forest_text(aes(y = studlab, label = n), data = studies,                    x = -0.35, hjust = 0.5) +   expand_limits(x = -0.45)

title: Getting started with ggmeta
text: Layout presets adapt plot common journal conventions. ordinary ggplot2 components, add +:  layout_bmj() layout_revman5() also available, plot ggplot can keep customising theme(), labs(), friends.
code: p <- ggforest(df, null_effect = 1) layout_jama(p)

title: Getting started with ggmeta
text: ggforest() back-transforms every summary measure correctly — exponentiation ratios, inverse-logit logit proportions, Fisher’s z correlations, . Single-group proportions rates get (meaningless) reference line:
code: prop <- metaprop(   event = c(15, 20, 12, 25), n = c(50, 60, 55, 70),   studlab = paste("Cohort", 1:4), sm = "PLOGIT" ) ggforest(prop)

title: Getting started with ggmeta
text: ggforest() returns ggplot, save like :
code: p <- ggforest(df, null_effect = 1) ggsave("forest.png", p, width = 7, height = 4, dpi = 300)

title: Getting started with ggmeta
text: See vignette("-meta-forest") side--side comparison meta::forest() tips reproducing output.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Hercules R. Freitas. Author, maintainer, copyright holder.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Freitas H (2026). ggmeta: Publication-Quality Forest Plots 'ggplot2'. R package version 0.1.0.9000, https://github.com/drhrf/ggmeta.
code: @Manual{,   title = {ggmeta: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with 'ggplot2'},   author = {Hercules R. Freitas},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0.9000},   url = {https://github.com/drhrf/ggmeta}, }

title: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with ggplot2
text: ggmeta builds publication-quality forest funnel plots ggplot2. Give meta object (meta package) plain tidy data frame — result ordinary ggplot can theme, compose, save.

title: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with ggplot2
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("drhrf/ggmeta")

title: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with ggplot2
text: meta package required — tidy data frame effect sizes standard errors enough. Set add_summary = TRUE pool studies fly (inverse-variance common effect DerSimonian–Laird random effects):
code: library(ggmeta)  studies <- data.frame(   studlab  = c("Trial 1", "Trial 2", "Trial 3", "Trial 4", "Trial 5"),   estimate = c(0.10, 0.35, 0.22, 0.48, 0.05),   se       = c(0.12, 0.10, 0.14, 0.16, 0.11) ) studies$ci_lower <- studies$estimate - 1.96 * studies$se studies$ci_upper <- studies$estimate + 1.96 * studies$se  ggforest(studies, add_summary = TRUE)

title: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with ggplot2
text: Pass meta object add columns = TRUE reproduce familiar meta::forest() table: effect estimate, 95% CI, weight column every study summary, headers, heterogeneity line (², τ², Q, p) — plain ggplot.  Everything optional. Choose columns show, toggle elements :
code: library(meta) #> Loading required package: metabook #> Loading 'meta' package (version 8.5-0). #> Type 'help(meta)' for a brief overview.  dat <- data.frame(   study   = c("Adams 2019", "Baker 2020", "Chen 2020",               "Diaz 2021", "Evans 2022", "Foster 2023"),   event.e = c(12,  8, 25, 18, 30, 15), n.e = c(120,  90, 200, 150, 250, 130),   event.c = c(20, 14, 30, 28, 35, 25), n.c = c(118,  92, 205, 148, 245, 128) )  m <- metabin(event.e, n.e, event.c, n.c,              data = dat, studlab = study, sm = "RR")  ggforest(m, columns = TRUE) ggforest(m, columns = c("estimate", "ci")) # only some columns ggforest(m, effect_header = "Risk ratio")  # rename the estimate column ggforest(m, show_hetstats = FALSE)          # hide the heterogeneity line ggforest(m, show_predict  = FALSE)          # hide the prediction interval ggforest(m, sort_studies  = FALSE)          # keep the input order

what: Forest and funnel plots on one canvas
title: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with ggplot2
text: ggmeta also draws funnel plots ggfunnel() (study effect vs. standard error, pseudo confidence-interval contours). every plot ordinary ggplot, forest funnel compose single figure patchwork — something awkward meta’s base-graphics output:
code: library(patchwork)  (ggforest(m) | ggfunnel(m)) +   plot_layout(widths = c(2, 1)) +   plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A")

title: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with ggplot2
text: Layout presets restyle plot common journals. ordinary ggplot2 components, add +:  layout_bmj() layout_revman5() also available.
code: layout_jama(ggforest(m, columns = TRUE))

title: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with ggplot2
text: column — sample sizes, events, anything — use geom_forest_text(), aligned study rows shared y. tidy_meta() exposes tidy data frame ggforest() builds internally, format_effect() builds "estimate (low high)" labels:
code: td      <- tidy_meta(m) studies <- td[!td$is_summary, ]  ggforest(m) +   geom_forest_text(aes(y = studlab, label = n.e), data = studies,                    x = 4, hjust = 0) +   expand_limits(x = 6)

title: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with ggplot2
text: ggplot2 native — add themes, layers, annotations, facets; save ggsave(). Forest funnel plots — ggforest() ggfunnel(), ordinary ggplots, can arrange together patchwork. meta::forest()-style tables — estimate / CI / weight columns heterogeneity caption, via columns = TRUE. Standalone meta — works tidy data frame meta object, can pool studies (add_summary = TRUE). Correct construction — every summary measure back-transformed right inverse (ratios, logit proportions, Fisher-z correlations, rates). Custom geometries — proper ggproto geoms CIs, summary diamonds, prediction intervals, reference lines, text columns. Journal styles — layout_jama(), layout_bmj(), layout_revman5().

title: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with ggplot2
text: vignette("getting-started") — tour package. vignette("-meta-forest") — coming meta::forest().

what: Format an effect estimate and confidence interval as text — format_effect
title: Format an effect estimate and confidence interval as text — format_effect
text: small helper building label geom_forest_text() column, producing strings "1.40 (0.65 3.00)".

title: Format an effect estimate and confidence interval as text — format_effect
code: format_effect(estimate, ci_lower, ci_upper, digits = 2, sep = " to ")

title: Format an effect estimate and confidence interval as text — format_effect
text: estimate, ci_lower, ci_upper Numeric vectors equal length. digits Number decimal places. Default 2. sep Separator confidence limits. Default " ".

title: Format an effect estimate and confidence interval as text — format_effect
text: character vector; NA estimates become empty strings.

title: Format an effect estimate and confidence interval as text — format_effect

what: Fortify a 'meta' object for use with 'ggplot2' — fortify.meta
title: Fortify a 'meta' object for use with 'ggplot2' — fortify.meta
text: S3 method ggplot2::fortify() converts meta objects tidy data frame. wrapper around tidy_meta().

title: Fortify a 'meta' object for use with 'ggplot2' — fortify.meta
code: # S3 method for class 'meta' fortify(model, data, ...)

title: Fortify a 'meta' object for use with 'ggplot2' — fortify.meta
text: model object class meta. data Ignored. Included S3 generic compatibility. ... Additional arguments passed tidy_meta().

title: Fortify a 'meta' object for use with 'ggplot2' — fortify.meta
text: data.frame returned tidy_meta().

title: Fortify a 'meta' object for use with 'ggplot2' — fortify.meta
code: # \donttest{ library(meta) #> Loading required package: metabook #> Loading 'meta' package (version 8.5-0). #> Type 'help(meta)' for a brief overview. library(ggplot2) m <- metabin(event.e, n.e, event.c, n.c,   data = data.frame(     event.e = c(14, 30), n.e = c(100, 150),     event.c = c(10, 25), n.c = c(100, 150)   ),   studlab = c("Study A", "Study B"),   sm = "RR" ) library(ggmeta) ggplot(fortify(m), aes(x = estimate, y = studlab)) +   geom_point()  # }

what: Study-level confidence intervals and point estimates for forest plots — geom_forest_ci
title: Study-level confidence intervals and point estimates for forest plots — geom_forest_ci
text: geom_forest_ci() draws confidence interval line weight-proportional square study forest plot. square area scales study weight, making precise studies visually prominent.

title: Study-level confidence intervals and point estimates for forest plots — geom_forest_ci
code: geom_forest_ci(   mapping = NULL,   data = NULL,   stat = "forest_ci",   position = "identity",   ...,   ci_width = 0.3,   point_size_range = c(1, 6),   na.rm = FALSE,   show.legend = NA,   inherit.aes = TRUE )

title: Study-level confidence intervals and point estimates for forest plots — geom_forest_ci
text: mapping Set aesthetic mappings created aes(). specified inherit.aes = TRUE (default), combined default mapping top level plot. must supply mapping plot mapping. data data displayed layer. three options: NULL, default, data inherited plot data specified call ggplot(). data.frame, object, override plot data. objects fortified produce data frame. See fortify() variables created. function called single argument, plot data. return value must data.frame, used layer data. function can created formula (e.g. ~ head(.x, 10)). stat statistical transformation use data layer. using geom_*() function construct layer, stat argument can used override default coupling geoms stats. stat argument accepts following: Stat ggproto subclass, example StatCount. string naming stat. give stat string, strip function name stat_ prefix. example, use stat_count(), give stat "count". information ways specify stat, see layer stat documentation. position position adjustment use data layer. can used various ways, including prevent overplotting improving display. position argument accepts following: result calling position function, position_jitter(). method allows passing extra arguments position. string naming position adjustment. give position string, strip function name position_ prefix. example, use position_jitter(), give position "jitter". information ways specify position, see layer position documentation. ... arguments passed ggplot2::layer(). often aesthetics, used set aesthetic fixed value, like colour = "red" linewidth = 1. ci_width Width CI line end-marks proportion spacing study rows. Default: 0.3. point_size_range Minimum maximum point size mm. Default: c(1, 6). na.rm FALSE (default), missing values removed warning. TRUE, missing values silently removed. show.legend logical. layer included legends? NA, default, includes aesthetics mapped. FALSE never includes, TRUE always includes. can also named logical vector finely select aesthetics display. include legend keys levels, even data exists, use TRUE.  NA, levels shown legend, unobserved levels omitted. inherit.aes FALSE, overrides default aesthetics, rather combining . useful helper functions define data aesthetics inherit behaviour default plot specification, e.g. annotation_borders().

title: Study-level confidence intervals and point estimates for forest plots — geom_forest_ci
text: geom_forest_ci() understands following aesthetics (required aesthetics bold): x — point estimate xmin — lower confidence limit xmax — upper confidence limit y — study position (usually factor) weight — study weight square sizing colour — line square border colour (default: "black") fill — square fill colour (default: "black") alpha — transparency (default: 1) linewidth — CI line width (default: 0.5) size — override square size; computed weight default linetype — CI line type (default: "solid")

title: Study-level confidence intervals and point estimates for forest plots — geom_forest_ci
code: library(ggplot2) df <- data.frame(   study = c("Study 1", "Study 2", "Study 3"),   estimate = c(0.5, 0.8, 0.3),   lower = c(0.2, 0.6, 0.1),   upper = c(0.8, 1.0, 0.5),   weight = c(1, 2, 0.5) ) ggplot(df, aes(y = study, x = estimate, xmin = lower, xmax = upper,                weight = weight)) +   geom_forest_ref() +   geom_forest_ci()

what: Summary effect diamond for forest plots — geom_forest_diamond
title: Summary effect diamond for forest plots — geom_forest_diamond
text: geom_forest_diamond() draws diamond (lozenge) shape representing summary effect estimate forest plot. left right tips mark confidence limits widest point marks estimate.

title: Summary effect diamond for forest plots — geom_forest_diamond
code: geom_forest_diamond(   mapping = NULL,   data = NULL,   stat = "forest_diamond",   position = "identity",   ...,   diamond_height = 0.4,   na.rm = FALSE,   show.legend = NA,   inherit.aes = TRUE )

title: Summary effect diamond for forest plots — geom_forest_diamond
text: mapping Set aesthetic mappings created aes(). specified inherit.aes = TRUE (default), combined default mapping top level plot. must supply mapping plot mapping. data data displayed layer. three options: NULL, default, data inherited plot data specified call ggplot(). data.frame, object, override plot data. objects fortified produce data frame. See fortify() variables created. function called single argument, plot data. return value must data.frame, used layer data. function can created formula (e.g. ~ head(.x, 10)). stat statistical transformation use data layer. using geom_*() function construct layer, stat argument can used override default coupling geoms stats. stat argument accepts following: Stat ggproto subclass, example StatCount. string naming stat. give stat string, strip function name stat_ prefix. example, use stat_count(), give stat "count". information ways specify stat, see layer stat documentation. position position adjustment use data layer. can used various ways, including prevent overplotting improving display. position argument accepts following: result calling position function, position_jitter(). method allows passing extra arguments position. string naming position adjustment. give position string, strip function name position_ prefix. example, use position_jitter(), give position "jitter". information ways specify position, see layer position documentation. ... arguments passed ggplot2::layer(). diamond_height Height diamond proportion spacing study rows. Default: 0.4. na.rm FALSE (default), missing values removed warning. TRUE, missing values silently removed. show.legend logical. layer included legends? NA, default, includes aesthetics mapped. FALSE never includes, TRUE always includes. can also named logical vector finely select aesthetics display. include legend keys levels, even data exists, use TRUE.  NA, levels shown legend, unobserved levels omitted. inherit.aes FALSE, overrides default aesthetics, rather combining . useful helper functions define data aesthetics inherit behaviour default plot specification, e.g. annotation_borders().

title: Summary effect diamond for forest plots — geom_forest_diamond
text: geom_forest_diamond() understands following aesthetics (required aesthetics bold): x — point estimate (diamond center) xmin — lower confidence limit (left tip) xmax — upper confidence limit (right tip) y — vertical position colour — diamond border colour (default: "black") fill — diamond fill colour (default: "black") alpha — fill transparency (default: 0.5) linewidth — border width (default: 0.5) linetype — border line type (default: "solid")

title: Summary effect diamond for forest plots — geom_forest_diamond
code: library(ggplot2) df <- data.frame(   model = c("Common effect", "Random effects"),   estimate = c(0.22, 0.22),   lower = c(-0.08, -0.08),   upper = c(0.52, 0.52) ) ggplot(df, aes(y = model, x = estimate, xmin = lower, xmax = upper)) +   geom_forest_diamond(aes(fill = model))

what: Prediction interval display for forest plots — geom_forest_predict
title: Prediction interval display for forest plots — geom_forest_predict
text: geom_forest_predict() draws prediction interval around random-effects summary estimate. prediction interval wider confidence interval represents range true effect new study expected fall.

title: Prediction interval display for forest plots — geom_forest_predict
code: geom_forest_predict(   mapping = NULL,   data = NULL,   stat = "forest_predict",   position = "identity",   ...,   cap_width = 0.32,   na.rm = FALSE,   show.legend = NA,   inherit.aes = TRUE )

title: Prediction interval display for forest plots — geom_forest_predict
text: mapping Set aesthetic mappings created aes(). specified inherit.aes = TRUE (default), combined default mapping top level plot. must supply mapping plot mapping. data data displayed layer. three options: NULL, default, data inherited plot data specified call ggplot(). data.frame, object, override plot data. objects fortified produce data frame. See fortify() variables created. function called single argument, plot data. return value must data.frame, used layer data. function can created formula (e.g. ~ head(.x, 10)). stat statistical transformation use data layer. using geom_*() function construct layer, stat argument can used override default coupling geoms stats. stat argument accepts following: Stat ggproto subclass, example StatCount. string naming stat. give stat string, strip function name stat_ prefix. example, use stat_count(), give stat "count". information ways specify stat, see layer stat documentation. position position adjustment use data layer. can used various ways, including prevent overplotting improving display. position argument accepts following: result calling position function, position_jitter(). method allows passing extra arguments position. string naming position adjustment. give position string, strip function name position_ prefix. example, use position_jitter(), give position "jitter". information ways specify position, see layer position documentation. ... arguments passed ggplot2::layer(). cap_width Width capped end-marks proportion row spacing. NA, levels shown legend, unobserved levels omitted. inherit.aes FALSE, overrides default aesthetics, rather combining . useful helper functions define data aesthetics inherit behaviour default plot specification, e.g. annotation_borders().

title: Prediction interval display for forest plots — geom_forest_predict
text: geom_forest_predict() understands following aesthetics (required aesthetics bold): x — point estimate (typically random effects estimate) xmin — lower prediction limit xmax — upper prediction limit y — vertical position colour — interval colour (default: "#6F9FBE") linewidth — interval line width (default: 0.45) linetype — interval line type (default: "dashed") alpha — transparency (default: 0.55)

title: Prediction interval display for forest plots — geom_forest_predict
code: library(ggplot2) df <- data.frame(   estimate = c(0.22),   lower = c(-0.27),   upper = c(0.70),   model = c("Random effects") ) ggplot(df, aes(y = model, x = estimate, xmin = lower, xmax = upper)) +   geom_forest_diamond() +   geom_forest_predict()

what: Reference line at null effect for forest plots — geom_forest_ref
title: Reference line at null effect for forest plots — geom_forest_ref
text: geom_forest_ref() draws vertical reference line null effect value — typically 0 difference measures (MD, SMD) 1 ratio measures (RR, , HR). line indicates treatment effect exists.

title: Reference line at null effect for forest plots — geom_forest_ref
code: geom_forest_ref(   mapping = NULL,   data = NULL,   ...,   xintercept = 0,   na.rm = FALSE,   show.legend = NA,   inherit.aes = TRUE )

title: Reference line at null effect for forest plots — geom_forest_ref
text: mapping Set aesthetic mappings created aes(). specified inherit.aes = TRUE (default), combined default mapping top level plot. must supply mapping plot mapping. data data displayed layer. three options: NULL, default, data inherited plot data specified call ggplot(). data.frame, object, override plot data. objects fortified produce data frame. See fortify() variables created. function called single argument, plot data. return value must data.frame, used layer data. function can created formula (e.g. ~ head(.x, 10)). ... arguments passed ggplot2::layer(). xintercept X-axis intercept reference line. Default: 0 (null effect difference measures). na.rm FALSE (default), missing values removed warning. TRUE, missing values silently removed. show.legend logical. layer included legends? NA, default, includes aesthetics mapped. FALSE never includes, TRUE always includes. can also named logical vector finely select aesthetics display. include legend keys levels, even data exists, use TRUE.  NA, levels shown legend, unobserved levels omitted. inherit.aes FALSE, overrides default aesthetics, rather combining . useful helper functions define data aesthetics inherit behaviour default plot specification, e.g. annotation_borders().

title: Reference line at null effect for forest plots — geom_forest_ref
text: geom_forest_ref() understands following aesthetics: colour — line colour (default: "gray50") linewidth — line width (default: 0.5) linetype — line type (default: "dashed") alpha — transparency (default: 0.8)

title: Reference line at null effect for forest plots — geom_forest_ref
code: library(ggplot2) df <- data.frame(   study = c("Study 1", "Study 2"),   estimate = c(0.5, 0.8),   lower = c(0.2, 0.6),   upper = c(0.8, 1.0),   weight = c(1, 2) ) ggplot(df, aes(y = study, x = estimate, xmin = lower,                xmax = upper, weight = weight)) +   geom_forest_ref(xintercept = 0) +   geom_forest_ci()

what: Add an aligned text column to a forest plot — geom_forest_text
title: Add an aligned text column to a forest plot — geom_forest_text
text: geom_forest_text() places column text labels aligned study rows forest plot — example event counts, sample sizes, weights, effect estimate rendered text. Rows align automatically shared y (study) aesthetic; horizontal position column set x argument. Place column beside data widening panel ggplot2::expand_limits().

title: Add an aligned text column to a forest plot — geom_forest_text
code: geom_forest_text(   mapping = NULL,   data = NULL,   stat = "identity",   position = "identity",   ...,   x = NULL,   hjust = 0,   size = 3.2,   na.rm = TRUE,   show.legend = FALSE,   inherit.aes = FALSE )

title: Add an aligned text column to a forest plot — geom_forest_text
text: mapping Set aesthetic mappings created aes(). specified inherit.aes = TRUE (default), combined default mapping top level plot. must supply mapping plot mapping. data data displayed layer. three options: NULL, default, data inherited plot data specified call ggplot(). data.frame, object, override plot data. objects fortified produce data frame. See fortify() variables created. function called single argument, plot data. return value must data.frame, used layer data. function can created formula (e.g. ~ head(.x, 10)). stat statistical transformation use data layer. using geom_*() function construct layer, stat argument can used override default coupling geoms stats. stat argument accepts following: Stat ggproto subclass, example StatCount. string naming stat. give stat string, strip function name stat_ prefix. example, use stat_count(), give stat "count". information ways specify stat, see layer stat documentation. position position adjustment use data layer. can used various ways, including prevent overplotting improving display. position argument accepts following: result calling position function, position_jitter(). method allows passing extra arguments position. string naming position adjustment. give position string, strip function name position_ prefix. example, use position_jitter(), give position "jitter". information ways specify position, see layer position documentation. ... arguments passed ggplot2::layer(), often used set aesthetic fixed value, e.g. colour = "grey30" fontface = "bold". x Fixed horizontal position column, x-axis data units. NULL, x must supplied mapping. hjust Horizontal justification. Default 0 (left-aligned), column reads cleanly x position rightward. size Text size millimetres. Default 3.2. na.rm TRUE (default), missing labels dropped silently. show.legend logical. layer included legends? NA, default, includes aesthetics mapped. FALSE never includes, TRUE always includes. can also named logical vector finely select aesthetics display. include legend keys levels, even data exists, use TRUE.  NA, levels shown legend, unobserved levels omitted. inherit.aes FALSE (default) layer inherit forest plot's x/xmin/xmax/weight mapping, y label need supplied.

title: Add an aligned text column to a forest plot — geom_forest_text
text: geom_forest_text() understands following aesthetics (required aesthetics bold): y — row position (map study variable forest layers) label — text display colour — text colour (default: "black") size — text size; set via size argument fontface, family, angle, alpha

title: Add an aligned text column to a forest plot — geom_forest_text
code: library(ggplot2) df <- data.frame(   study = c("A", "B", "C"),   estimate = c(0.5, 0.8, 0.3),   lower = c(0.2, 0.6, 0.1),   upper = c(0.8, 1.0, 0.5),   n = c(120, 240, 90) ) ggplot(df, aes(y = study, x = estimate, xmin = lower, xmax = upper)) +   geom_forest_ci() +   geom_forest_text(aes(y = study, label = n), x = 1.15) +   expand_limits(x = 1.25)

what: Pseudo-confidence-interval contours for a funnel plot — geom_funnel_contour
title: Pseudo-confidence-interval contours for a funnel plot — geom_funnel_contour
text: geom_funnel_contour() draws funnel-shaped pseudo confidence interval(s) around reference effect. study standard error se, expected effect bias lies within centre +/- z * se, contours straight lines fanning apex se = 0. funnel plot reversed standard-error axis form familiar funnel.

title: Pseudo-confidence-interval contours for a funnel plot — geom_funnel_contour
code: geom_funnel_contour(   centre,   se_max,   level = 0.95,   ...,   colour = "grey55",   linetype = "dashed",   linewidth = 0.4,   alpha = NA )

title: Pseudo-confidence-interval contours for a funnel plot — geom_funnel_contour
text: centre Reference effect (usually pooled estimate) funnel centred , analysis scale. se_max Largest standard error contours reach (bottom funnel). level Confidence level(s) contour(s), e.g. 0.95 c(0.95, 0.99). Default 0.95. ... arguments passed ggplot2::layer(). colour, linetype, linewidth, alpha Line appearance.

title: Pseudo-confidence-interval contours for a funnel plot — geom_funnel_contour
text: ggplot2 layer.

title: Pseudo-confidence-interval contours for a funnel plot — geom_funnel_contour
text: layer builds data centre, se_max, level, use plot's data; add plot maps effect x standard error y.

title: Pseudo-confidence-interval contours for a funnel plot — geom_funnel_contour
code: library(ggplot2) studies <- data.frame(   estimate = c(-0.5, -0.2, -0.4, 0.1, -0.3),   se       = c(0.10, 0.30, 0.15, 0.35, 0.22) ) ggplot(studies, aes(x = estimate, y = se)) +   geom_funnel_contour(centre = -0.3, se_max = 0.4) +   geom_point() +   scale_y_reverse()

title: Create a forest plot — ggforest
text: primary function creating publication-quality forest plots. Accepts meta object (created meta::metabin(), meta::metacont(), etc.) tidy data frame (returned tidy_meta() constructed manually).

title: Create a forest plot — ggforest
code: ggforest(x, ...)  # Default S3 method ggforest(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'meta' ggforest(   x,   ...,   back_trans = c("auto", "exp", "none"),   sort_studies = TRUE,   show_summary = TRUE,   show_predict = TRUE,   show_hetstats = TRUE,   null_effect = NULL,   xlab = NULL )  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' ggforest(   x,   null_effect = 0,   xlab = "Effect (95% CI)",   ylab = NULL,   title = NULL,   caption = NULL,   add_summary = FALSE,   summary_method = c("common", "random"),   level = 0.95,   columns = NULL,   effect_header = NULL,   ... )

title: Create a forest plot — ggforest
text: x meta object tidy data frame columns estimate, ci_lower, ci_upper, studlab, optionally weight, is_summary, summary_type, subgroup. ... Additional arguments passed methods. back_trans Back-transform ratio measures? "auto" (default), "exp", "none". sort_studies Sort studies effect estimate? Default: TRUE. show_summary Include summary effect rows? Default: TRUE. show_predict Include prediction interval? Default: TRUE (random effects model present). show_hetstats Show heterogeneity statistics plot caption? Default: TRUE. null_effect Null effect value reference line. Default: 0. xlab X-axis label. Default: "Effect (95% CI)". ylab Y-axis label. Default: NULL (label — study labels serve y-axis text). title Plot title. Default: NULL. caption Plot caption. Default: NULL. add_summary data-frame method, TRUE compute pooled summary study rows (inverse-variance /DerSimonian-Laird) draw diamond — --fly meta-analysis without meta package. Needs se column, ci_lower/ci_upper recover . Default: FALSE. summary_method pooled summaries add add_summary = TRUE: "common", "random", (default). level Confidence level pooled summary interval. Default 0.95. columns Add meta::forest()-style table text columns right plot. TRUE shows effect estimate, 95% CI, weight; pass subset/order c("estimate", "ci"). NULL (default) draws columns. effect_header Header estimate column (e.g. "Hedges' g"). Defaults summary measure (e.g. "SMD", "RR").

title: Create a forest plot — ggforest
text: ggplot object. Add standard ggplot2 layers (themes, scales, labels) customize.

title: Create a forest plot — ggforest
code: # \donttest{ library(meta) m <- metabin(event.e, n.e, event.c, n.c,   data = data.frame(     event.e = c(14, 30, 15, 22),     n.e     = c(100, 150, 100, 120),     event.c = c(10, 25, 12, 18),     n.c     = c(100, 150, 100, 120)   ),   studlab = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D"),   sm = "RR" ) ggforest(m)  # }

what: Funnel plot from a meta-analysis — ggfunnel
title: Funnel plot from a meta-analysis — ggfunnel
text: ggfunnel() draws funnel plot: study's effect estimate standard error (measure precision), pseudo confidence-interval contours around pooled effect. Asymmetry can signal small-study effects publication bias. Like ggforest(), accepts meta object tidy data frame returns ordinary ggplot, forest funnel plot compose one canvas , example, patchwork.

title: Funnel plot from a meta-analysis — ggfunnel
code: ggfunnel(x, ...)  # Default S3 method ggfunnel(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'meta' ggfunnel(x, ..., ref = c("common", "random"), level = 0.95)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' ggfunnel(   x,   centre = NULL,   sm = NULL,   level = 0.95,   xlab = NULL,   ylab = "Standard error",   title = NULL,   ... )

title: Funnel plot from a meta-analysis — ggfunnel
text: x meta object, data frame estimate se columns (study effects standard errors analysis scale). ... Additional arguments passed methods. ref pooled estimate centre funnel : "common" (fixed effect, default) "random". level Confidence level(s) funnel contours, e.g. 0.95 c(0.95, 0.99). Default 0.95. centre Effect funnel centred . Defaults inverse-variance (common-effect) estimate supplied studies. sm Summary measure (e.g. "RR", "PLOGIT"), used label x-axis , transformed measures (ratios, proportions, rates, correlations), show back-transformed axis labels. Optional. xlab, ylab Axis labels. ylab defaults "Standard error". title Plot title. Default NULL.

title: Funnel plot from a meta-analysis — ggfunnel
text: ggplot object.

title: Funnel plot from a meta-analysis — ggfunnel
code: # \donttest{ library(meta) m <- metabin(event.e, n.e, event.c, n.c,   data = data.frame(     event.e = c(12, 8, 25, 18, 30, 15), n.e = c(120, 90, 200, 150, 250, 130),     event.c = c(20, 14, 30, 28, 35, 25), n.c = c(118, 92, 205, 148, 245, 128)   ),   studlab = paste("Study", 1:6), sm = "RR" ) ggfunnel(m)  # }

what: ggmeta: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with 'ggplot2' — ggmeta-package
title: ggmeta: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with 'ggplot2' — ggmeta-package
text: 'ggplot2' extension creating publication-quality forest plots meta package objects tidy data frames. Provides custom ggproto geometries study-level confidence intervals, summary diamonds, prediction intervals, null-effect reference lines.

title: ggmeta: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with 'ggplot2' — ggmeta-package
text: ggforest primary entry point. Pass either meta object (meta) tidy data frame.

title: ggmeta: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with 'ggplot2' — ggmeta-package
text: geom_forest_ci — study-level CI weight-proportional squares geom_forest_diamond — summary effect diamond polygon geom_forest_ref — vertical null-effect reference line geom_forest_predict — prediction interval display

title: ggmeta: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with 'ggplot2' — ggmeta-package
text: tidy_meta converts meta objects tidy data frames suitable use ggmeta geoms direct ggplot2 plotting.

title: ggmeta: Publication-Quality Forest Plots with 'ggplot2' — ggmeta-package
text: Maintainer: Hercules R. Freitas hercules.freitas@uerj.br (ORCID) [copyright holder] Authors: Hercules R. Freitas hercules.freitas@uerj.br (ORCID) [copyright holder]

text: Modifies forest plot match BMJ (British Medical Journal) style conventions.

code: layout_bmj(p)

code: layout_revman5(p)

text: ggplot object RevMan5-style formatting applied.

title: Forest plot theme — theme_forest
text: complete ggplot2 theme optimized forest plots. Removes unnecessary grid lines, adjusts margins, sets sensible defaults forest plot aesthetics.

title: Forest plot theme — theme_forest
code: theme_forest(   base_size = 11,   base_family = "",   base_line_size = base_size/22,   base_rect_size = base_size/22 )

title: Forest plot theme — theme_forest
text: base_size Base font size pts. Default: 11. base_family Base font family. Default: "". base_line_size Base line size. Default: base_size / 22. base_rect_size Base rect size. Default: base_size / 22.

title: Forest plot theme — theme_forest
code: library(ggplot2) df <- data.frame(   study = c("Study 1", "Study 2"),   estimate = c(0.5, 0.3),   lower = c(0.2, 0.1),   upper = c(0.8, 0.5),   weight = c(1, 2) ) ggplot(df, aes(y = study, x = estimate, xmin = lower,                xmax = upper, weight = weight)) +   geom_forest_ref() +   geom_forest_ci() +   theme_forest()

title: Funnel plot theme — theme_funnel
text: light ggplot2 theme ggfunnel() plots.

title: Funnel plot theme — theme_funnel
code: theme_funnel(base_size = 11, base_family = "")

title: Funnel plot theme — theme_funnel
text: base_size Base font size pts. Default 11. base_family Base font family. Default "".

title: Funnel plot theme — theme_funnel
text: ggplot2 theme.

title: Funnel plot theme — theme_funnel
code: library(ggplot2) df <- data.frame(estimate = c(-0.3, 0.1, -0.2), se = c(0.1, 0.3, 0.2)) ggplot(df, aes(estimate, se)) +   geom_point() +   scale_y_reverse() +   theme_funnel()

what: Tidy a 'meta' object into a plottable data frame — tidy_meta
title: Tidy a 'meta' object into a plottable data frame — tidy_meta
text: Converts objects class meta (meta package) tidy data frame suitable use ggmeta geometries ggforest(). returned data frame one row per study summary.

title: Tidy a 'meta' object into a plottable data frame — tidy_meta
code: tidy_meta(x, ...)  # Default S3 method tidy_meta(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' tidy_meta(   x,   add_summary = FALSE,   summary_method = c("common", "random"),   level = 0.95,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'meta' tidy_meta(   x,   back_trans = c("auto", "exp", "none"),   sort_studies = TRUE,   add_summary = TRUE,   add_predict = TRUE,   add_subgroups = TRUE,   ... )

title: Tidy a 'meta' object into a plottable data frame — tidy_meta
text: x object class meta, e.g. created meta::metabin(), meta::metacont(), meta::metagen(). ... Additional arguments passed methods. add_summary data-frame method, TRUE compute inverse-variance / DerSimonian-Laird pooled summary study rows append (--fly meta-analysis, meta package required). Needs se column, ci_lower/ci_upper recover . Default FALSE. summary_method pooled summaries add add_summary = TRUE: "common", "random", (default). level Confidence level pooled summary interval. Default 0.95. back_trans estimate confidence limits back-transformed natural scale? "auto" (default), summary measure back-transformed correct inverse via meta::backtransf() (exponentiation ratios, inverse-logit PLOGIT, Fisher's z correlation ZCOR, etc.); linear measures left unchanged. Use "exp" force exponentiation "none" keep analysis scale. sort_studies TRUE (default), sort studies effect estimate (favorable top). add_predict TRUE (default), include prediction interval row available. add_subgroups TRUE (default), include subgroup headers within-group summary rows subgroups present.

title: Tidy a 'meta' object into a plottable data frame — tidy_meta
text: data.frame one row per study summary, columns: studlab Study label (character) estimate Point estimate (numeric) ci_lower Lower confidence limit (numeric) ci_upper Upper confidence limit (numeric) se Standard error (numeric) weight Study weight (numeric, NA summaries) p_value P-value (numeric) n Sample size person-time (numeric, optional) event Number events (numeric, optional) is_summary Logical, TRUE summary rows summary_type Character: "none", "common", "random", "subgroup", "predict" subgroup Subgroup label (character NA) returned data frame following attributes: sm summary measure type (e.g. "RR", "", "MD") null_effect null effect value reference line method meta-analysis method common logical, TRUE common effect model used random logical, TRUE random effects model used tau heterogeneity estimate tau k number studies

title: Tidy a 'meta' object into a plottable data frame — tidy_meta
code: # \donttest{ library(meta) m <- metabin(event.e, n.e, event.c, n.c,   data = data.frame(     event.e = c(14, 30), n.e = c(100, 150),     event.c = c(10, 25), n.c = c(100, 150)   ),   studlab = c("Study A", "Study B"),   sm = "RR" ) tidy_meta(m) #>                    studlab estimate   ci_lower  ci_upper        se    weight #> 1                  Study A 1.400000 0.65296960  3.001671 0.3891382  6.603774 #> 2                  Study B 1.200000 0.74247238  1.939466 0.2449490 16.666667 #> common       Common effect 1.257143 0.83734105  1.887413 0.2073331        NA #> random      Random effects 1.253660 0.83507644  1.882058 0.2072992        NA #> 11     Prediction interval 1.253660 0.09000235 17.462462 0.2072992        NA #>          p_value is_summary summary_type subgroup #> 1      0.3872255      FALSE         none     <NA> #> 2      0.4566801      FALSE         none     <NA> #> common 0.2697065       TRUE       common     <NA> #> random 0.2754777       TRUE       random     <NA> #> 11            NA       TRUE      predict     <NA> # }

title: ggmeta 0.1.0.9000
text: New ggfunnel() draws funnel plot (study effect vs. standard error pseudo confidence-interval contours) meta object tidy data frame, returning ordinary ggplot. Ratio, proportion, rate, correlation measures drawn analysis scale labelled back-transformed values. ggforest() ggfunnel() ggplots, forest funnel plot compose one canvas  — e.g. ggforest(m) | ggfunnel(m). Comes geom_funnel_contour() theme_funnel(). ggforest() gains columns draw meta::forest()-style table text columns (effect estimate, 95% CI, weight) headers right plot: ggforest(m, columns = TRUE), subset columns = c("estimate", "ci"). estimate header can set effect_header. Positions computed axis’s space, columns line linear log (ratio) axes. ggforest() tidy_meta() gain add_summary (summary_method level) pool tidy data frame effect sizes fly — inverse-variance common effect DerSimonian–Laird random effect — summary diamond can drawn without meta package. New geom_forest_text() adds custom text column aligned study rows, plus format_effect() build "estimate (low high)" labels.

title: ggmeta 0.1.0.9000
text: Refreshed default look: solid null-effect line dotted “consensus” line pooled estimate (meta::forest()), terracotta / dark-blue summary diamonds, panel gridlines, (columns) bold headers x-axis title centred plot region. Summary-diamond legends now read “Common effect” / “Random effects” / “Subgroup (common)” / “Subgroup (random)” instead raw keys, non-informative square-size legend hidden. Getting-started “Coming meta::forest()” vignettes added.

title: ggmeta 0.1.0.9000
text: geom_forest_ref() now maps intercept x-axis transformation, null-effect consensus lines land correct place ratio (log) axes — previously null line RR//HR rendered ten times intended value. Back-transformation delegates meta::backtransf(), every summary measure gets correct inverse (inverse-logit PLOGIT, arcsine PAS, Fisher’s z ZCOR, exp rates IRLN). Previously proportions, rates, correlations exponentiated incorrectly. Single-group proportions rates longer draw null-effect reference line log x-axis (detect_null_effect() now returns NA ). Subgroup forest plots keep study grouped subgroup header, headers render plain text instead literal **markdown**. Study confidence intervals longer dropped object types whose weights unavailable (e.g. metaprop, metarate); weights fall back inverse variance weight longer required aesthetic. Forest plots use stable, conventional orientation (studies top, summary diamond bottom) regardless optional layers present. heterogeneity caption drawn plotmath expression, fixing example-rendering error non-UTF-8 graphics devices.
